claims.claim_text: The module opens with a detailed 2018 travel-booking deployment disaster involving 11 hours of impact, 23,000 phantom bookings, and more than $6 million in losses.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In 2018, a well-known travel booking platform deployed a database migration to production on a Friday afternoon. The deployment was manual -- an engineer ran a script on a bastion host, copy-pasting commands from a wiki page that had not been updated in four months. The script applied schema changes in the wrong order, corrupting a foreign key relationship that silently broke booking confirmations. For 11 hours over the weekend, customers completed bookings and received confirmation emails, but no actual reservations were created. The company had to manually reconcile 23,000 phantom bookings, issue refunds, and rebook customers at higher prices. The estimated cost exceeded $6 million, not counting the permanent loss of trust from affected travelers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Manual production deployments and unreviewed database migrations can cause severe customer-facing failures, data inconsistencies, and expensive cleanup work. CI/CD pipelines reduce that risk by making deployments repeatable, testable, and easier to roll back.
claims.rationale: This is a precise named-style incident anecdote with specific numbers but no attributable source, so the false specifics should be removed rather than softened.

claims.claim_text: OIDC federation is a modern security pattern for avoiding long-lived AWS access keys in external CI systems such as GitHub Actions.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: AWS IAM documentation directly recommends OIDC federation instead of storing long-term credentials in external applications.

claims.claim_text: CodePipeline is described as one of the first fully managed CI/CD services in any cloud, and the module claims most AWS teams previously ran Jenkins on EC2.
claims.anchor_text: - **AWS CodePipeline was one of the first fully managed CI/CD services** in any cloud, launching in July 2015. Before that, most AWS teams ran Jenkins on EC2 instances -- a pattern that still exists but is increasingly replaced by managed alternatives.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **AWS CodePipeline became generally available in July 2015.** Before AWS-native managed CI/CD services were widely used, many teams ran tools such as Jenkins on EC2 instances.
claims.rationale: The July 2015 launch is citable, but the market-ranking and 'most AWS teams' claims are too broad for an honest inline citation as written.

claims.claim_text: The module gives specific monthly cost comparisons for CodeBuild versus an always-on Jenkins server.
claims.anchor_text: - **CodeBuild runs on managed compute** that scales to zero when idle. Unlike Jenkins, where you pay for the build server 24/7, CodeBuild charges only for build minutes. A typical small team spends $5-15/month on CodeBuild versus $50-150/month for an always-on Jenkins instance.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **CodeBuild runs on managed compute** and charges for build usage rather than requiring you to keep a dedicated build server running. In a small setup, that can be much cheaper than operating an always-on Jenkins host, depending on build volume and infrastructure choices.
claims.rationale: The pricing lesson is sound, but the exact team-spend ranges are illustrative and not stably citable as a universal fact.

claims.claim_text: The OIDC bullet says GitHub's OIDC provider issues tokens valid for about 15 minutes, cites AWS documentation from 2021, and says the pattern has become the standard.
claims.anchor_text: - **OIDC federation for GitHub Actions** eliminates the need for IAM access keys entirely. GitHub's OIDC provider issues short-lived tokens (valid for about 15 minutes) that AWS trusts directly. This pattern, documented by AWS in 2021, has become the standard for GitHub-to-AWS authentication.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **OIDC federation for GitHub Actions** avoids storing long-lived IAM access keys in GitHub. GitHub Actions can request short-lived identity tokens, and AWS can trust those tokens to issue temporary credentials for a tightly scoped IAM role.
claims.rationale: The exact token lifetime and 'has become the standard' framing are not safely supported here, but the underlying security guidance is.

claims.claim_text: The ECS blue/green bullet says CodeDeploy is required, ECS has no native blue/green controller, and CodeDeploy remains the only option for blue/green traffic shifting and rollback controls.
claims.anchor_text: - **Blue/green deployments on ECS** require AWS CodeDeploy — there is no native ECS blue/green deployment controller. The ECS Deployment Circuit Breaker (introduced 2020) provides automated rollbacks for rolling updates only. CodeDeploy remains the only option for blue/green with traffic shifting controls (linear, canary, all-at-once) and automatic rollback on CloudWatch alarm triggers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Blue/green deployments on ECS** can be implemented with CodeDeploy, while the ECS deployment circuit breaker handles rollback for rolling deployments. AWS has also added newer ECS-native deployment strategies, so check the current ECS deployment documentation before choosing an approach.
claims.rationale: As written, this capability statement is outdated against current ECS deployment documentation and should be rewritten rather than cited.

claims.claim_text: The module says CodeBuild phases run in order and that post_build still runs after failures if on-failure is configured a certain way.
claims.span_hint: section: CodeBuild, text after buildspec example
claims.anchor_text: **Phases** execute in order: `install` -> `pre_build` -> `build` -> `post_build`. If any command in a phase fails (non-zero exit code), the build fails and subsequent phases are skipped (except `post_build`, which runs even on failure if you set `on-failure: CONTINUE`).
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Phases** execute in order: `install` -> `pre_build` -> `build` -> `post_build`. If a command fails, that phase fails, so later steps that must not run after a failure should be guarded explicitly.
claims.rationale: The ordering is straightforward, but the conditional post_build behavior is too imprecise as written for a clean authoritative citation.

claims.claim_text: CodeBuild environment variables can come from inline values, SSM Parameter Store, and Secrets Manager.
claims.span_hint: section: CodeBuild, explanatory bullets after buildspec
claims.rationale: The CodeBuild API reference directly documents PLAINTEXT, PARAMETER_STORE, and SECRETS_MANAGER environment variable types.

claims.claim_text: The imagedefinitions.json artifact is the special file CodePipeline ECS standard deployments use to map container names to image URIs.
claims.span_hint: section: CodeBuild, explanatory bullets after buildspec
claims.rationale: AWS CodePipeline documentation directly describes imagedefinitions.json as the input for ECS standard deployment actions.

claims.claim_text: CodeBuild caching speeds later builds by reusing stored components such as custom cached directories or Docker layers.
claims.span_hint: section: CodeBuild, explanatory bullets after buildspec
claims.rationale: AWS documentation explicitly states that caching stores reusable build components and saves build time across builds.

claims.claim_text: Building Docker images inside CodeBuild requires privileged mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a CodeBuild Project, sentence after CLI example
claims.rationale: The CodeBuild project documentation states that privileged mode must be enabled for builds that need Docker daemon access.

claims.claim_text: The SMALL CodeBuild compute row lists 3 vCPU, 3 GB, and $0.005/min.
claims.span_hint: section: Build Compute Types, table row BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL
claims.suggested_rewrite: | BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL | 2 | 4 GiB | See current AWS pricing |
claims.rationale: The current CodeBuild documentation does not match this row's compute values, and pricing should point readers to the live pricing page.

claims.claim_text: The MEDIUM CodeBuild compute row lists 7 vCPU, 15 GB, and $0.010/min.
claims.span_hint: section: Build Compute Types, table row BUILD_GENERAL1_MEDIUM
claims.suggested_rewrite: | BUILD_GENERAL1_MEDIUM | 4 | 8 GiB | See current AWS pricing |
claims.rationale: The current CodeBuild documentation does not match this row's compute values, and pricing should point readers to the live pricing page.

claims.claim_text: The LARGE CodeBuild compute row lists 15 vCPU, 72 GB, and $0.020/min.
claims.span_hint: section: Build Compute Types, table row BUILD_GENERAL1_LARGE
claims.suggested_rewrite: | BUILD_GENERAL1_LARGE | 8 | 16 GiB | See current AWS pricing |
claims.rationale: The current CodeBuild documentation does not match this row's compute values, and pricing should point readers to the live pricing page.

claims.claim_text: The 2XLARGE CodeBuild compute row lists 72 vCPU, 145 GB, and $0.040/min.
claims.span_hint: section: Build Compute Types, table row BUILD_GENERAL1_2XLARGE
claims.suggested_rewrite: | BUILD_GENERAL1_2XLARGE | 72 | 144 GiB | See current AWS pricing |
claims.rationale: The current documentation uses 144 GiB for this compute type, and the price column should defer to the live pricing page.

claims.claim_text: CodeDeploy supports deployments to EC2, on-premises servers, Lambda functions, and ECS services.
claims.span_hint: section: CodeDeploy: Deployment Strategies, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The CodeDeploy overview page directly lists these supported deployment targets.

claims.claim_text: The module says any failed ECS lifecycle hook automatically triggers a rollback.
claims.span_hint: section: The appspec.yml File, sentence after hook example
claims.anchor_text: If any hook function returns failure, CodeDeploy rolls back automatically.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a hook function reports failure, the deployment fails, and CodeDeploy rolls back automatically only when automatic rollback is enabled for the deployment or deployment group.
claims.rationale: AWS documents that rollback on validation failure depends on rollback configuration, so the unconditional statement is too strong.

claims.claim_text: The modern recommended CodePipeline integration for GitHub is the GitHub App/CodeConnections source action rather than the older OAuth-token-plus-webhook source action.
claims.span_hint: section: Source Providers: CodeStar Connections vs Webhooks
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents the GitHub App action as recommended and the OAuth app action as not recommended.

claims.claim_text: The trust policy's sub condition should scope access to a specific repository and branch for GitHub Actions OIDC.
claims.span_hint: section: OIDC Federation for GitHub Actions, paragraph after workflow example
claims.rationale: The official aws-actions repository shows the AWS trust-policy pattern that restricts the token.actions.githubusercontent.com sub claim to a specific repo and branch.

further_reading.title: AWS CodeBuild buildspec reference
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for phases, artifacts, caching syntax, reports, and buildspec behavior.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECS blue/green deployments
further_reading.why_relevant: Current ECS deployment guidance, including modern blue/green terminology and lifecycle behavior.

further_reading.title: AWS IAM OIDC federation
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary AWS guidance for replacing long-lived external credentials with OIDC-based temporary role assumptions.

notes: Quiz content, hands-on exercise steps, code blocks, and solution snippets were excluded per the prompt. Several ECS deployment statements in the narrative are outdated as of April 23, 2026 and were marked for rewrite instead of citation.
